function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The xAI merger became effective February 2, 2026 and the X merger March 28, 2025. For how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis, and what analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create?
I need to examine the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the merger timeline, fiscal year structure, and the retrospective recast accounting treatment. Let me parse the filing first.
steps.reasoning_content:
Let me analyze this question carefully. The user is asking about:

1. For how many months of FY2025 was each entity (xAI and X Holdings) included on an actual-close basis?
2. What analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create?

Let me think about the dates:
- xAI merger became effective February 2, 2026
- X Holdings merger became effective March 28, 2025

FY2025 would be January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025 (assuming calendar year fiscal year).

On an actual-close basis (not recast):
- X Holdings: merged March 28, 2025, so it would be included from March 28, 2025 to December 31, 2025. That's approximately 9 months (April through December = 9 months, or more precisely about 9 months and 3 days).
- xAI: merged February 2, 2026, so it was NOT included in FY2025 on an actual-close basis at all. It would be 0 months in FY2025.

But wait - the filing context says the consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include both xAI and X Holdings for all historical periods presented. So on a recast basis, both entities appear in all periods. But on an actual-close basis, the question is about when they were actually merged.

Let me retrieve the actual filing to get the precise details about the merger dates, fiscal year, and the recast accounting treatment.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.message: Now let me retrieve specific information about the merger accounting treatment and the recast basis.
steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing has been parsed. Now let me retrieve information about the merger dates, the recast accounting, and the fiscal year structure.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the merger accounting treatment for xAI and X Holdings, including effective dates, common-control accounting recast, and how historical periods are presented in {{spacex_s1}}
Retrieve the fiscal year end date and fiscal year definition for SpaceX in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided prospectus excerpts, here is the detailed accounting treatment, effective dates, and historical presentation for the mergers involving SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings:

### 1. Effective Dates of the Mergers
*   **The "X Merger"**: Effective **March 28, 2025**, xAI completed its acquisition of X Holdings Corp. (“X”) and X.AI Corp., making them wholly-owned subsidiaries of xAI.
*   **The "xAI Merger"**: Effective **February 2, 2026**, SpaceX completed its acquisition of X.AI Holdings Corp. (“xAI”), making xAI a wholly-owned subsidiary of SpaceX. 

### 2. Common-Control Accounting Treatment & Recast
*   **Common-Control Reorganization**: The mergers were accounted for as reorganizations of entities under **common control**. This is because Elon Musk held a controlling financial and majority voting interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented.
*   **Retrospective Combination**: The consolidated financial statements were retrospectively recast to combine the net assets of all entities at their **historical carrying amounts** for all periods presented.
*   **No Goodwill or Intangibles**: Because this was a common-control transaction, no new goodwill or other intangible assets were recorded.
*   **Intercompany Eliminations**: All historical related-party transactions and intercompany balances between SpaceX, xAI, and X were eliminated in consolidation.
*   **Change in Reporting Entity**: This accounting presentation constitutes a formal "change in reporting entity." As a result, separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided.
*   **Equity Continuation**: Historical capital stock and shareholders’ equity reflect a continuation of SpaceX's historical equity, combined with the historical equity of X and xAI (adjusted by the respective merger exchange ratios). 

### 3. Presentation of Historical Periods
*   **Common Stock Share Counts**: All historical shares of X, X.AI Corp., and xAI Common Stock are presented in the financial statements **as if** they had already been converted into SpaceX Common Stock at the applicable exchange ratios for all periods presented up to the xAI Merger date. These are fully included in SpaceX's historical common stock share counts.
*   **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock**: Unlike the common stock, xAI's historical Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted basis for periods prior to the February 2, 2026 merger date. Because it was a legally distinct equity class of a predecessor entity, it is presented in historical balance sheets consistent with its legal form and rights at the time. The actual conversion into SpaceX Common Stock is recognized prospectively and presented separately in the Q1 2026 Statement of Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock and Shareholders’ Equity.
*   **Customer Warrants**: xAI warrants outstanding at the time of the merger featured an acceleration clause. They were automatically exercised on a cashless basis and converted into fully vested SpaceX Class A Common Stock upon the merger's closing.
*   **2026 Stock Split Adjustment**: On May 4, 2026, SpaceX effected a 5-for-1 forward stock split. All historical share and per-share information across all periods presented in the prospectus has been **retroactively adjusted** to reflect this split.

Based on the provided text, here is the information regarding SpaceX's fiscal year:

* **Fiscal Year End Date:** December 31 
*(Evidenced by multiple references to the "Year Ended December 31" in the financial tables and the phrase "following December 31 of the fiscal year".)*
* **Fiscal Year Definition:** The provided text does not contain an explicit, formal definition of the fiscal year. However, contextually, it is clear that SpaceX operates on a standard **calendar year** basis, with the fiscal year beginning on January 1 and ending on December 31.

steps.message: Let me retrieve more details about segment reporting and any specific analytical challenges discussed in the filing.
steps.reasoning_content: Now I have the key information. Let me also retrieve more details about the analytical challenges of retrospective recast accounting, and any specific disclosures about segment reporting or how the historical financials are presented.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve segment reporting information, including how segments are defined and how historical segment financial data is presented for xAI, X, Starlink, and launch services in {{spacex_s1}}

Retrieve any discussion of the challenges, limitations, or analytical considerations related to retrospective recast accounting, common-control transactions, or comparability of historical financial statements in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided financial statements and notes, here is the segment reporting information regarding how the segments are defined (including the placement of xAI, X, Starlink, and launch services) and how historical financial data is presented:

### 1. Segment Definitions
Following the mergers, the Company’s Chief Executive Officer (acting as the Chief Operating Decision Maker, or CODM) organizes the company into **three operating and reportable segments**:

*   **Space Segment:** Includes **Launch Services** and Launch & Development. This segment designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets (such as Falcon and Starship) to provide access to space. *Note: Space segment revenue only reflects third-party customer launches and government activities.*
*   **Connectivity Segment:** Includes **Starlink** (Consumer, Enterprise & Government, and Starlink Mobile). This segment operates the worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband satellite network in Low-Earth Orbit.
*   **AI Segment:** Includes **xAI** and **X** (formerly Twitter). This segment operates a vertically integrated AI platform spanning the frontier LLM "Grok," AI solutions, AI computational infrastructure, and X (the real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform). Revenue categories include Advertising and AI Solutions & Infrastructure.

### 2. Presentation of Historical Segment Financial Data
Because of the complex merger timeline involving entities owned by Elon Musk, the historical segment data is presented using specific accounting treatments:

*   **Common-Control Reorganizations & Retrospective Combination:** 
    *   xAI acquired X Holdings Corp. (X) in March 2025, and SpaceX subsequently acquired xAI in February 2026. 
    *   Because Elon Musk held a controlling financial/majority voting interest in all these entities during the presented periods, these transactions were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**.
    *   Consequently, the historical financial data is presented as a **retrospective combination** of the net assets at their historical carrying amounts for all periods presented. No new goodwill or intangible assets were recorded, and all historical related-party transactions between the entities were eliminated in consolidation.
*   **Recasting of Prior Periods:** Following the mergers, the company evaluated its historical segment structure and realigned it into the current three-segment model (Space, Connectivity, AI) effective Q1 2026. **Prior period presentations for segments have been conformed/recast** to match this current reporting structure so that historical data is directly comparable across the Space, Connectivity, and AI segments.
*   **Inter-Segment Eliminations (Starlink Launches):** The Space segment launches many of the satellites used by the Connectivity segment. However, the company **does not recognize any inter-segment revenue** for Starlink launches. Instead, these launch costs are capitalized within "Property, plant, and equipment, net" as satellite assets. 
*   **Performance Evaluation Metric:** The CODM assesses performance and allocates resources based on **Segment Income (Loss) from Operations** (comparing actuals to historical results and forecasts). The company also tracks and reports *Segment Adjusted EBITDA* as a supplemental non-GAAP measure to evaluate profitability and cash flow generation across the segments.

Based on the provided text, the discussion of challenges, limitations, and analytical considerations regarding retrospective recast accounting, common-control transactions, and the comparability of historical financial statements can be categorized into the following key areas:

### 1. Specific Limitations on Retrospective Recasting (The Preferred Stock Exception)
The most significant accounting limitation and analytical challenge detailed in the text relates to the inconsistent retrospective treatment of different equity classes:
*   **No Retrospective Recast for Preferred Stock:** While the common stock of X and xAI is presented historically *as if* it had been converted into SpaceX Common Stock at the applicable exchange rates for all periods, **xAI’s Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is NOT recast on an as-converted basis** for periods prior to the merger. 
*   **Analytical Consideration/Reasoning:** This limitation exists because the preferred stock was legally outstanding during all historical periods and represented a "separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity." 
*   **Impact on Comparability:** Consequently, historical consolidated balance sheets and statements of equity reflect the xAI Preferred Stock in its original legal form and rights prior to the merger date. The conversion into SpaceX Common Stock is only recognized prospectively in the period the merger actually closed (Q1 2026). This creates a structural break in the historical comparability of the company's capital structure and shareholders' equity.

### 2. Analytical Considerations for Common-Control Transactions
The text outlines several strict accounting rules applied to the mergers of SpaceX, xAI, and X (all controlled by Elon Musk), which impact how historical data must be analyzed:
*   **No Step-Up in Basis or New Goodwill:** The retrospective combination reflects the net assets of the entities at their **historical carrying amounts**. No new goodwill or other intangible assets were recorded as a result of the mergers.
*   **Elimination of Historical Related-Party Transactions:** All historical related-party transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X have been eliminated in consolidation. Analysts must consider that historical top-line revenue or expense figures may look different than the sum of the previously standalone entities due to these eliminations.
*   **Change in Reporting Entity:** The retrospective combination legally constitutes a "change in reporting entity," requiring the financial statements to be viewed as a single continuous economic entity rather than an acquirer and acquiree.
*   **Absence of Separate Financial Statements:** Because the consolidated financial statements already reflect the common-control reorganization for all periods presented, **separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided**. This limits an analyst's ability to evaluate the standalone historical financial health, cash flows, or operational margins of the individual predecessor entities prior to the mergers.

### 3. Limitations on Predictive Value and Data Comparability
The preamble includes standard but critical warnings regarding the utility of the retrospectively recast historical data:
*   **Not Indicative of Future Results:** The summary historical consolidated financial and operating data is explicitly stated as "not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period." The integration of vastly different business models (aerospace, AI, and social media/free speech platforms) under one umbrella means historical combined data may not reflect future synergies, integration costs, or combined operational realities.
*   **Interim vs. Full-Year Limitations:** Results for interim periods (e.g., the three months ended March 31, 2026, and 2025) are not necessarily indicative of full fiscal year results.
*   **Summary Data Limitations:** The summary historical data is "not intended to replace the consolidated financial statements" and is qualified in its entirety by the full statements and notes. 

### 4. Audit and Reporting Considerations
*   **Dual-Dating of the Auditor's Report:** The independent auditor's report highlights a specific procedural consideration regarding retrospective recasts. The audit opinion is dated March 30, 2026, **except** for the effects of the common-control reorganization, the stock split, and the change in reportable segments, for which the date is **May 7, 2026**. This indicates that the retrospective recasting and segment realignments required significant subsequent audit procedures and distinct evaluation periods separate from the standard year-end/quarter-end audit fieldwork.
*   **Retroactive Stock Split Adjustments:** A five-for-one stock split effective May 4, 2026, required all historical share and per-share information (including the conversion rates of Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock) to be retroactively adjusted across all periods presented to maintain comparability in per-share metrics.

steps.message: Now let me retrieve any additional details about the specific months and the intercompany eliminations to ensure precision.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have comprehensive information to answer the question. Let me synthesize:

**FY2025 = January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025** (calendar year fiscal year)

**On an actual-close basis (not recast):**

1. **X Holdings (X):** Merger effective March 28, 2025. So X was included from March 28, 2025 to December 31, 2025. That's approximately 9 months (April through December = 9 full months, plus 3 days in March). So roughly 9 months.

2. **xAI:** Merger effective February 2, 2026. So xAI was NOT included in FY2025 on an actual-close basis at all. That's 0 months in FY2025.

Wait, but I need to think about this more carefully. The X merger was completed by xAI (xAI acquired X Holdings on March 28, 2025). Then SpaceX acquired xAI on February 2, 2026. So:

- On an actual-close basis for SpaceX's FY2025 consolidated financials:
  - Neither xAI nor X Holdings were part of SpaceX in FY2025 on an actual-close basis
  - xAI merged into SpaceX on February 2, 2026 (after FY2025 ended)
  - X Holdings merged into xAI on March 28, 2025 (but xAI itself wasn't part of SpaceX until February 2, 2026)

Wait, but the question asks "for how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis." This could mean:
- How many months was each entity included in the SpaceX consolidated entity on an actual-close basis?
- Or how many months was each entity included in the combined entity (however defined)?

Given the context of the question about retrospective recast accounting, I think the question is asking about the actual-close basis vs. the recast basis. On an actual-close basis:

- X Holdings merged into xAI on March 28, 2025. So X was part of xAI from April-December 2025 (9 months).
- xAI merged into SpaceX on February 2, 2026. So xAI (including X) was NOT part of SpaceX during FY2025 at all.

But wait - the recast accounting combines all entities for all periods. So the question is really about the difference between the actual-close timeline and the recast presentation.

Let me reconsider. The question says "For how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis." This is asking about the actual legal merger dates relative to FY2025 (Jan 1 - Dec 31, 2025):

1. **X Holdings:** Merged into xAI on March 28, 2025. So on an actual-close basis, X Holdings was included in the xAI consolidated entity for approximately 9 months of FY2025 (April through December, or more precisely March 28 to December 31 = ~9.1 months).

2. **xAI:** Merged into SpaceX on February 2, 2026. So on an actual-close basis, xAI was included in SpaceX for 0 months of FY2025 (the merger happened after FY2025 ended).

But wait - the question might be asking about inclusion in the SpaceX consolidated entity specifically. In that case:
- Neither entity was included in SpaceX on an actual-close basis during FY2025
- X Holdings was part of xAI for ~9 months of FY2025
- xAI became part of SpaceX in February 2026

Actually, I think the question is more nuanced. Let me re-read: "For how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis, and what analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create?"

The question is asking about the actual-close basis for each entity in FY2025. Given the recast accounting presents all entities as if they were combined for all periods, the analytical challenge is that the recast presentation doesn't match the actual economic reality of when the entities were combined.

Let me think about this differently. The recast accounting presents SpaceX, xAI, and X as a single entity for all periods. But on an actual-close basis:

- **SpaceX standalone:** Existed for all 12 months of FY2025
- **X Holdings:** Merged into xAI on March 28, 2025, so it was part of xAI for ~9 months of FY2025 (April-December). But it was NOT part of SpaceX at all during FY2025.
- **xAI:** Merged into SpaceX on February 2, 2026, so it was NOT part of SpaceX during any month of FY2025.

So on an actual-close basis relative to the SpaceX consolidated entity:
- xAI: 0 months of FY2025
- X Holdings: 0 months of FY2025 (it was part of xAI, not SpaceX)

But the recast accounting presents both as if they were part of SpaceX for all 12 months of FY2025.

Actually, I think the question might be asking about the combined entity more broadly. Let me reconsider:

The X merger (xAI acquiring X Holdings) was effective March 28, 2025. So X Holdings was included in the xAI consolidated entity for approximately 9 months of FY2025 (April through December).

The xAI merger (SpaceX acquiring xAI) was effective February 2, 2026. So xAI was included in SpaceX for 0 months of FY2025.

The analytical challenge: The retrospective recast accounting presents all three entities (SpaceX, xAI, X) as a single consolidated entity for ALL periods presented, including all 12 months of FY2025. This creates a disconnect between the reported financials (which show 12 months of combined operations) and the actual economic reality (where the entities were separate for significant portions of FY2025). This makes it difficult to:
1. Assess the true historical performance of the combined entity
2. Compare period-over-period growth rates meaningfully
3. Evaluate standalone performance of individual entities
4. The combined historical data may not be indicative of future results

Let me also consider: the question says "each entity" - so it's asking about xAI and X Holdings separately.

For FY2025 (January 1 - December 31, 2025):
- **X Holdings:** Merged into xAI on March 28, 2025. On an actual-close basis, X was included in the xAI consolidated group for approximately 9 months (April-December 2025). It was NOT part of SpaceX at all during FY2025.
- **xAI:** Merged into SpaceX on February 2, 2026. On an actual-close basis, xAI was NOT part of SpaceX during any month of FY2025 (0 months).

But in the recast financials, both entities appear as if they were part of SpaceX for all 12 months of FY2025.

The analytical challenge is that the recast financials create a "fictional" 12-month combined entity for FY2025, when in reality:
- SpaceX was standalone for all 12 months
- xAI was standalone for all 12 months (not part of SpaceX)
- X Holdings was standalone for ~3 months (Jan-Mar 2025), then part of xAI for ~9 months

This makes year-over-year comparisons, trend analysis, and performance evaluation challenging because the recast numbers don't reflect the actual economic integration timeline.

Let me now formulate my answer clearly.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the exact effective dates of the X merger and xAI merger, and any discussion of how the periods prior to the mergers are presented, including any carve-out or standalone financial information in {{spacex_s1}}
Retrieve the auditor's report dates, including any dual-dating related to the common-control reorganization and retrospective recast in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the exact effective dates of the mergers and the details regarding how the periods prior to the mergers are presented:

### **Exact Effective Dates**
*   **X Merger:** **March 28, 2025** (xAI completed its acquisition of X Holdings Corp. and X.AI Corp.)
*   **xAI Merger:** **February 2, 2026** (SpaceX completed its acquisition of X.AI Holdings Corp.)

### **Presentation of Periods Prior to the Mergers**
The text does **not** discuss or present "carve-out" or "standalone" financial information. Instead, because the transactions were between entities under common control (Elon Musk held a majority voting interest in all entities), the company applies a **retrospective combination** approach. 

The specific accounting and presentation treatments for prior periods include:
*   **Retrospective Combination & Change in Reporting Entity:** The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to combine the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts. This presentation constitutes a "change in reporting entity."
*   **Equity & Share Counts:** Historical capital stock and shareholders’ equity reflect a continuation of historical SpaceX equity combined with the historical equity of X and xAI, adjusted by the respective exchange ratios used to effect the mergers. All historical common shares of X, X.AI Corp., and xAI are presented as if they had been converted into SpaceX Common Stock for all periods.
*   **Eliminations & Goodwill:** No new goodwill or other intangible assets were recorded. All historical related-party transactions between the entities were eliminated in consolidation.
*   **Exception for xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock:** Unlike the common stock, xAI’s historical Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is **not** recast on an as-converted basis for periods prior to the xAI Merger. Because it represented a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity, it is presented in the historical balance sheets consistent with its legal form and rights during those prior periods. The conversion into SpaceX Common Stock is only recognized prospectively in the period the merger actually occurred (Q1 2026). 

In summary, rather than providing standalone or carve-out financials for the predecessor entities, the company retroactively restates all historical periods to present the combined entity as if the common-control reorganization had always been in place.

Based on the provided text from the Independent Auditor's Report and related notes, here are the details regarding the audit report dates, dual-dating exceptions, and the auditor's tenure:

### **Auditor's Report Dates & Dual-Dating**
* **Base Report Date:** March 30, 2026
* **Dual-Dated Exceptions (May 7, 2026):** The audit report date is extended to **May 7, 2026** specifically for the following retrospective recasts and adjustments:
  * The effects of the reorganization of entities under common control.
  * The effects of the stock split (as discussed in Note 1 to the consolidated financial statements).
  * The change in reportable segments (as discussed in Note 19 to the consolidated financial statements).
*(Note: The text also confirms May 7, 2026, as the date the consolidated financial statements were available to be issued and the cutoff for evaluating subsequent events).*

### **Auditor's Tenure**
* The auditing firm has served as the Company’s independent auditor continuously since **2012**.

steps.message: Now I have all the information needed to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me calculate the precise months.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me synthesize the answer.

**FY2025 = January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025** (calendar year fiscal year)

**On an actual-close basis:**

1. **X Holdings (X):** The X merger (xAI acquiring X Holdings) became effective March 28, 2025. So on an actual-close basis, X Holdings was included in the xAI consolidated entity from March 28, 2025 through December 31, 2025. That's approximately 9 months (April through December = 9 full months, plus 3 days in March). Let me be more precise: March 28 to December 31 = 279 days, which is about 9.17 months. But conventionally, we'd say approximately 9 months.

2. **xAI:** The xAI merger (SpaceX acquiring xAI) became effective February 2, 2026. So on an actual-close basis, xAI was NOT included in SpaceX's consolidated entity during any month of FY2025. That's 0 months.

But wait - the question asks about "each entity" being included. Let me think about what "included" means here. The recast accounting presents all three entities as a single consolidated entity for all periods. So the question is asking: on an actual-close basis (i.e., when the legal mergers actually happened), how many months of FY2025 was each entity actually part of the consolidated SpaceX entity?

- **X Holdings:** Was acquired by xAI on March 28, 2025. But xAI itself wasn't part of SpaceX until February 2, 2026. So X Holdings was never actually part of SpaceX during FY2025 on an actual-close basis. However, X Holdings was part of xAI for ~9 months of FY2025.

- **xAI:** Was acquired by SpaceX on February 2, 2026. So xAI was not part of SpaceX during any month of FY2025 on an actual-close basis. 0 months.

So on an actual-close basis relative to the SpaceX consolidated entity:
- xAI: 0 months of FY2025
- X Holdings: 0 months of FY2025 (it was part of xAI, not SpaceX, during FY2025)

But the recast accounting presents both as if they were part of SpaceX for all 12 months of FY2025.

Actually, I think the question might be asking about the combined entity more broadly - i.e., when was each entity actually included in whatever consolidated group it became part of:

- X Holdings: Included in xAI from March 28, 2025 → ~9 months of FY2025
- xAI: Included in SpaceX from February 2, 2026 → 0 months of FY2025

This interpretation makes more sense because it highlights the analytical challenge: the recast financials show 12 months of combined operations for FY2025, but in reality:
- X Holdings was only combined with xAI for ~9 months
- xAI was not combined with SpaceX at all during FY2025

The analytical challenge is that the retrospective recast creates a "pro forma-like" presentation that doesn't reflect the actual economic integration timeline, making it difficult to:
1. Assess true period-over-period growth
2. Evaluate standalone performance
3. Understand the actual operational integration
4. The combined historical data may not be indicative of future results

Let me now write my final answer.

So from March 28 to December 31, 2025 is 278 days. Let me convert that to months: 278/30.44 ≈ 9.13 months. Conventionally, we'd say approximately 9 months (April through December = 9 full months, plus 3 days in March).

Now let me write my final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Actual-Close Basis: Months of FY2025 Inclusion

SpaceX's fiscal year ends December 31, so FY2025 runs from **January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025**.

### X Holdings (X)
- **Merger effective date:** March 28, 2025 (xAI acquired X Holdings Corp.)
- **Actual-close inclusion in FY2025:** X Holdings was included in the xAI consolidated entity from March 28 through December 31, 2025 — approximately **9 months** of FY2025 (278 days, or ~9.1 months). It was standalone for the first ~3 months of FY2025 (January 1 – March 27).
- **Inclusion in SpaceX:** X Holdings was **never** directly part of SpaceX during FY2025 on an actual-close basis, because xAI itself did not merge into SpaceX until February 2, 2026.

### xAI
- **Merger effective date:** February 2, 2026 (SpaceX acquired X.AI Holdings Corp.)
- **Actual-close inclusion in FY2025:** xAI was included in SpaceX for **0 months** of FY2025. The merger closed after FY2025 ended entirely.

### Summary Table

| Entity | Actual-Close Months in FY2025 (within SpaceX consolidated) | Recast Months in FY2025 |
|---|---|---|
| SpaceX (standalone) | 12 months | 12 months |
| xAI | **0 months** | 12 months (recast) |
| X Holdings | **0 months** (within SpaceX); ~9 months (within xAI) | 12 months (recast) |

---

## Analytical Challenge Created by Retrospective Recast Accounting

The retrospective recast under common-control accounting creates a fundamental **disconnect between reported financials and actual economic integration timing**. The key analytical challenges are:

### 1. Phantom Full-Year Combination
The recast financials present SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings as a single consolidated entity for **all 12 months of FY2025** — and indeed for all historical periods presented. In reality, neither xAI nor X Holdings was legally part of SpaceX during any month of FY2025. The FY2025 consolidated revenue, expenses, and cash flows therefore reflect a combined entity that did not actually exist during that period.

### 2. Loss of Standalone Visibility
Because the recast constitutes a "change in reporting entity," **separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided**. Analysts cannot evaluate the standalone historical financial health, cash flows, or operational margins of the individual predecessor entities prior to the mergers. This is particularly significant given the vastly different business models (aerospace, AI/LLM, and social media/advertising).

### 3. Distorted Period-over-Period Comparisons
Year-over-year growth rates and trend analyses are unreliable because the degree of "fictional" combination varies by period. For example, FY2024 recast financials show all three entities combined for 12 months, yet none were legally combined at any point during that year. The recast numbers embed intercompany eliminations of historical related-party transactions that would not have been eliminated in the standalone entities' actual financials.

### 4. Inconsistent Equity Treatment
While common stock is retrospectively recast on an as-converted basis for all periods, **xAI's Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is NOT recast** for periods prior to February 2, 2026. This creates a structural break in the historical comparability of the capital structure — the balance sheet shows preferred stock in its original legal form for prior periods, but common stock as if already converted.

### 5. No Goodwill or Step-Up in Basis
The common-control accounting means net assets are carried at **historical carrying amounts** with no goodwill or intangible asset recognition. This understates the economic consideration that would have been paid in an arm's-length transaction and eliminates the amortization/depreciation step-ups that would normally accompany a business combination, potentially inflating reported post-merger profitability relative to a fair-value acquisition.

### 6. Audit Complexity
The auditor's report is **dual-dated** — March 30, 2026 for the base financials, but **May 7, 2026** for the effects of the common-control reorganization, stock split, and segment realignment — indicating that the retrospective recast required significant additional audit procedures and judgment beyond standard fieldwork.

**Bottom line:** No period in the S-1 reflects standalone SpaceX operations. Every historical figure is a recast combination of entities that were legally and operationally separate for significant portions of the periods presented, making it analytically hazardous to draw conclusions about organic growth, margin trends, or capital efficiency without disaggregating the segment-level data (Space, Connectivity, AI) with caution.

---

      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}
